title: Cooking with Herbs
content:
HERBS (Fresh preferred when available)

General Rule: Add delicate herbs at end of cooking; use sparingly; don't combine too many kinds at once. Substitution: ¼ teaspoon powdered or ½ teaspoon crushed dried = 1 tablespoon fresh chopped. Reconstitute dried herbs in liquid 10-60 minutes before using.

Basil: Affinity for tomatoes, fish, eggs. Called "l'herbe royale." Darkens quickly after cutting. Roots in water; keep fresh sprigs in water on counter. Dries poorly; keep below 110°F.

Bay: Use with discretion—¼ fresh leaf or ⅛ dried leaf per quart. Stocks, sauces, marinades, Bouquet Garni. Dry in August. Do not confuse edible Laurus nobilis with poisonous cherry laurel (Prunus laurocerasus).

Chervil: Delicate "fines herbes" component. With chicken, veal, omelets, salads, spinach, Béarnaise sauce. Self-sowing annual; needs shade to prevent purple discoloration.

Chives: With soft white cheese, eggs, green sauces. Cut low on plant; keep blooms picked. Do not store uncooked with chives longer than overnight (becomes unpleasantly strong).

Dill: Seed and leaf in sour cream, fish, beans, cucumbers, cabbage, potato salad. Do not brown butter if using for dill sauce.

Fennel: Leaves and seeds with fish, lentils, rice, potatoes, apple pies. Leaves do not retain flavor dried.

Marjoram/Oregano: Pungent; with sausages, stews, tomato dishes, lamb, pork, chicken, eggs, pizza, cabbage, green beans. Sweet marjoram (Origanum marjoram) tender perennial; pot marjoram (O. onite) and wild oregano (O. vulgare) hardy perennials.

Mint: Peppermint, spearmint, apple, orange, pineapple varieties. In fruit cups, coleslaw, peas, zucchini, lamb, veal, cream cheeses, chocolate, teas, jellies. Fresh: ¼-½ teaspoon crushed per serving. 1 drop oil often too much.

Parsley: High vitamin A. Blends other herb flavors; destroys garlic/onion scent. Curled for garnish; Italian/uncurled for cooking. Root of Hamburg type cooked like parsnips.

Rosemary: Resinous, pungent—use sparingly. Lamb, duck, partridge, rabbit, capon, veal, peas, spinach, pizza. ⅛-¼ teaspoon fresh for 4 servings in marinades.

Sage: With fatty meats (pork, sausages), duck, goose, rabbit, cheese, chowders. Fresh chopped leaves superior to dried. Dry carefully—thick leaves mold easily.

Savory: Winter (resinous, evergreen) in stews, stuffings, meat loaves. Summer (delicate) in green beans, green bean salad, horseradish sauce, lentil soup, deviled eggs, fat fish, pork, potatoes, tomatoes, French dressing.

Tarragon: Fresh is luxury; flavor largely lost in drying. Vinegar-preserve: 3 tablespoons leaves per quart mild vinegar. Essential in Béarnaise. Too pungent for soups. With eggs, mushrooms, tomatoes, sweetbreads, tartare and mustard sauces, fish, chicken.

Thyme: With poultry, mutton, veal, pork, rabbit; Creole and gumbo; brown sauces; pickled beets and tomatoes; fat fish, stews, stuffings, vegetables; stocks. Many varieties; grows well in rock gardens.

Fines Herbes: Equal parts parsley, tarragon, chives, chervil. Minced and added at last minute.

Bouquet Garni: 3-4 sprigs parsley or chervil, ⅛-½ bay leaf, 2 sprigs fresh thyme (optional: leek white, 2 cloves). Bind with string or wrap in celery ribs. Add for last 30 minutes of cooking.
